text.title: Requirements Prioritization — Methods and Selection Decision Tree
text.summary: Six prioritization methods with mechanics, known failure modes, and a 6-step selection decision tree based on data availability.

rule.statement: Walk the following steps in order; first match wins; document the path and all rejected methods with rationale. Refuse to recommend a method when its prerequisites are missing — return null and request the data.
rule.rationale: Method drift mid-run (silently switching RICE to weighted-sum because RICE "felt arbitrary") destroys credibility; locking the method first and scoring after is the only way to prevent gaming.

list.item: 1. Release-scope (categorical) decision → MoSCoW with 60/20/20 effort budget enforced
2. SAFe or PI planning context → WSJF (Cost-of-Delay / Job Size, floor Job Size at 1 unit)
3. Kano survey data available (≥ 30 respondents) → Kano; refuse if no survey data
4. Reach baseline metric available + item count ≥ 30 → RICE; default Confidence to 0.8
5. Regulated / audited, explicit criteria weights needed → weighted-sum (cap at 7 criteria, lock weights before scoring)
6. Item count < 30 or no effort estimates → Value-Effort matrix (quick triage only)
7. None match → stop; request missing data; do not invent it

rule.statement: Enforce the 60/20/20 effort budget mechanically: Must ≤ 60% of capacity; Should ≈ 20%; Could ≈ 20%. Reject any run where Must exceeds the budget.
rule.rationale: Without the budget constraint the labels are meaningless — every stakeholder makes their items Must.

antipattern.description: Treating MoSCoW as ordinal ranking within categories.
antipattern.reason: MoSCoW answers "will it ship", not "in what order"; pair with stack rank inside each bucket for sequence.

rule.statement: Default Confidence to 0.8. Force 1.0 only with a written primary-source link (analytics, prior A/B, customer interview transcript). Calibrate Impact by quartile rank before mapping to the 0.25/0.5/1/2/3 scale.
rule.rationale: LLM optimism anchors Confidence at 100% and Impact at 1, making the formula degenerate to Reach/Effort and hiding meaningful differences.

antipattern.description: Reach scored as a round number with no source URL.
antipattern.reason: Tag every row with reach_estimated_no_baseline if no source is cited; prevents "10,000 users" invented by optimism.

rule.statement: Apply Kano only when survey data from ≥ 30 respondents using functional + dysfunctional question pairs is available. Conference-room Kano without survey data is guessing labels.
rule.rationale: Kano categories (Basic, Performance, Delighter, Indifferent, Reverse) change over time — today's Delighter becomes tomorrow's Basic; re-run every 6-12 months.

rule.statement: Floor WSJF Job Size at 1 unit. Calibrate each Cost-of-Delay component (User-Business Value, Time Criticality, Risk Reduction & Opportunity Enablement) with separate evidence; do not let one person score all three components for the same item.
rule.rationale: Without a Job Size floor, tiny items dominate the queue. Correlated CoD components produce a noisier version of RICE rather than a meaningful sequencing tool.

rule.statement: Tie every row to a REQ-XXX ID before scoring. Include a status-quo "do nothing" baseline row with effort 0 and current value. Re-run on a trigger (sprint review, quarter end, major new candidate) not on schedule alone.
rule.rationale: Free-text rows orphan when the rank changes; a status-quo baseline reveals when the entire backlog scores below it — a signal the rank is answering the wrong question.

rule.statement: Run a sensitivity check: perturb the most leverage-heavy input by ±20% (RICE: Confidence; weighted-sum: top-3 weights; WSJF: Job Size) and mark items whose rank moves more than 5 positions as low_confidence_rank.
rule.rationale: Items fragile to small input perturbations should not drive large resource commitments without additional data collection.

text.reference: RICE scoring table with data-quality-flag column
